function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
For each segment, explain how incremental kilograms delivered to orbit ultimately translate into revenue generation and identify when revenue recognition occurs. Calculate the Connectivity-to-Space revenue ratio for FY2025 and Q1 2026 and interpret the trend.

steps.reasoning_content:

2. Identify when revenue recognition occurs for each segment
3. Calculate the Connectivity-to-Space revenue ratio for FY2025 and Q1 2026
4. Interpret the trend

I need to first parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the segment information, revenue recognition policies, and financial data.

Let me start by parsing the filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about the segments, revenue recognition policies, and financial data from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What are the business segments of SpaceX described in {{spacex_s1}}? List each segment and describe how they generate revenue.
What is the revenue recognition policy for each segment in {{spacex_s1}}? When does SpaceX recognize revenue for launch services and connectivity services?
What are the segment revenues for FY2025 and Q1 2026 in {{spacex_s1}}? Provide the revenue breakdown by segment including Space and Connectivity segments.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial statements and notes, SpaceX operates through **three reportable business segments**: **Space**, **Connectivity**, and **AI**. 

Here is a breakdown of each segment and how they generate revenue:

### 1. Space Segment
This segment designs, manufactures, and launches reusable rockets (such as Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, and the developing Starship) and spacecraft (Dragon) to provide high-cadence, reliable, and affordable access to space.
*   **How it generates revenue:** Revenue is generated primarily through launch and mission services provided to **commercial and government customers**. It is disaggregated into two main categories:
    *   **Launch Services:** Revenue generated from deploying customer payloads to their intended orbits (LEO, MEO, GEO, lunar, interplanetary, or the ISS). This revenue is typically recognized at a **"point in time"** (upon launch or deployment) due to the interchangeability of flight hardware and minimal unique engineering costs.
    *   **Launch & Development:** Revenue derived from long-term, fixed-price contracts (ranging from one to fourteen years) for the development of spacecraft and provision of mission services for government agency space programs. This revenue is recognized **"over time"** using a cost-to-cost input method.
*   *Note on Internal Launches:* The Space segment allocates a significant amount of launch capacity to SpaceX's internal Connectivity and AI segments (e.g., launching Starlink satellites). However, **no inter-segment revenue is recognized** for these internal launches; Space segment revenue *only* reflects external customer launches and activities.

### 2. Connectivity Segment
This segment operates a worldwide high-speed, low-latency broadband network powered by thousands of Starlink satellites in Low-Earth Orbit (LEO), delivering internet and communications to millions of users globally.
*   **How it generates revenue:** Revenue is generated through the **Starlink** offering and is disaggregated into the following categories:
    *   **Consumer:** Broadband internet subscriptions and services for individual consumers, including those in remote areas.
    *   **Enterprise & Government:** Connectivity solutions tailored for businesses and government entities. This category also includes **Starlink Mobile** (satellite-to-mobile texting and voice services for mobile users).
    *   **Products:** All hardware/product revenue (such as Starlink user terminals/dishes) is attributed entirely to the Connectivity segment.

### 3. AI Segment
This segment operates a vertically integrated AI platform that spans frontier Large Language Models (LLM), consumer/enterprise AI solutions, computational infrastructure, and the social media platform X.
*   **How it generates revenue:** Revenue is disaggregated into two primary categories:
    *   **Advertising:** Revenue generated from the X platform (described as a real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform).
    *   **AI Solutions & Infrastructure:** Revenue generated from AI products like **Grok Business** and **Grok Enterprise**, which cater to specific workflows and security needs. These are sold using **license-based, consumption-based, or outcome-based pricing models**. It also includes revenue from AI computational infrastructure. 

***

**Summary of Segment Synergy:**
SpaceX's business model relies on extreme vertical integration. The **Space** segment acts as the foundational backbone, providing the launch capacity required to deploy the **Connectivity** segment's Starlink constellation and, increasingly, the orbital data centers/infrastructure required for the **AI** segment. While Space drives the physical infrastructure, the Connectivity and AI segments are highlighted as the primary drivers of near-term revenue growth and scale.

Based on the provided financial text, here is the revenue recognition policy for each segment, including exactly when revenue is recognized for launch and connectivity services:

### **1. Space Segment**
*   **Launch Services:** Recognized at a **point in time**. Because flight hardware is interchangeable and requires minimal unique engineering, revenue and costs are deferred and **not recognized until the launch or deployment** of the customer’s payload/spacecraft to its intended orbit.
*   **Launch and Development:** Recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method**. This applies to government and development contracts where the company's performance creates an asset with no alternative use and it has an enforceable right to payment. Revenue is recorded based on costs incurred to date (e.g., materials, labor hours) relative to the total estimated cost at completion.
*   **Internal Starlink Launches:** No inter-segment revenue is recognized for launching internal Starlink satellites. Instead, these launch costs are capitalized as Property, Plant, and Equipment (satellites) and depreciated over time.

### **2. Connectivity Segment**
*   **Broadband and Mobile Connectivity Services:** Recognized **over time** as the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits. Advanced payments are recorded as deferred revenue and recognized either ratably over the subscription term (e.g., month-to-month consumer billing) or based on actual data consumption.
*   **Starlink Kits (Hardware/Products):** Recognized at a **point in time** upon delivery to the customer, or upon installation for certain enterprise customers. (This is reported net of sales returns and credits).
*   **Starshield (Government/National Security Networks):** Recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method** for multi-year fixed-price contracts.
*   **Multiple Performance Obligations:** For customer contracts that include both hardware (Starlink Kit) and services (broadband), the transaction price is allocated to each distinct obligation based on its standalone selling price.

### **3. AI Segment**
*   *Note:* While the AI segment (AI Solutions & Infrastructure) is listed in the revenue disaggregation tables and is noted as a future recipient of launch capacity allocations, the provided text **does not detail** the specific revenue recognition policy for the AI segment.

---

### **Summary: When does SpaceX recognize revenue for these specific services?**
*   **Launch Services:** Revenue is recognized at a **point in time**, specifically **upon the successful launch or deployment** of the customer's spacecraft/payload to its intended orbit. 
*   **Connectivity Services:** Revenue is recognized **over time**, continuously as the customer uses the service. It is recognized either **ratably over the subscription term** (for fixed monthly fees) or **based on actual data consumption** (for variable-priced services).

Based on the provided financial summaries and tables, here is the revenue breakdown by segment for FY2025 and Q1 2026:

**FY2025 (Year Ended December 31, 2025)**
*   **Connectivity:** $11,387 million
*   **Space:** $4,086 million
*   **AI:** $3,201 million
*   **Total Consolidated Revenue:** $18,674 million

**Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026)**
*   **Connectivity:** $3,257 million
*   **AI:** $818 million
*   **Space:** $619 million
*   **Total Consolidated Revenue:** $4,694 million

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the segment revenue data. Let me also retrieve more details about how kilograms to orbit translate into revenue for each segment, and any additional details about the revenue model.

Let me calculate the Connectivity-to-Space revenue ratios:

FY2025: Connectivity / Space = $11,387 / $4,086 = 2.79
Q1 2026: Connectivity / Space = $3,257 / $619 = 5.26

arguments.prompt: How does SpaceX describe the relationship between kilograms delivered to orbit and revenue generation in {{spacex_s1}}? What is the role of launch capacity in enabling Starlink constellation deployment and revenue?
What are the revenue recognition policies for the AI segment in {{spacex_s1}}? How does SpaceX recognize revenue for advertising, AI solutions, and X platform?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, SpaceX describes the relationship between kilograms delivered to orbit ("Mass to Orbit") and revenue generation as a divergence between raw operational scale and recognized Space segment revenue, driven by the company's internal use of its launch capacity. 

Here is how SpaceX defines and quantifies this relationship:

### 1. Definition and Purpose of "Mass to Orbit"
*   **The Metric:** "Mass to orbit" is defined as the total kilograms of payload deployed to orbit in a given period. It includes verified mass from Starlink satellites, customer payloads, and development cargo from all successful orbital and flight tests (excluding failed or scrubbed attempts).
*   **Indicator of Scale:** It serves as a primary key performance metric to measure the company's operational scale, throughput, capacity, and scalability. 

### 2. The Divergence Between Mass to Orbit and Space Segment Revenue
SpaceX explicitly notes that **mass to orbit and launch cadence grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue**. This happens for a specific accounting and operational reason:
*   **Exclusion of Inter-Segment Revenue:** A massive portion of the mass delivered to orbit consists of internal Starlink constellation deployments. SpaceX **does not recognize inter-segment revenue** for these internal launches. 
*   **Capitalization of Costs:** Instead of generating Space segment revenue, the costs of launching Starlink satellites are capitalized as assets ("satellites in Property, plant, and equipment, net") within the Connectivity segment and depreciated over time.
*   **Customer-Only Revenue Recognition:** Space segment revenue *only* reflects launches and activities for third-party commercial and government customers. Because the internal business "continues to absorb most of the growth in our launch capacity," Space segment revenue growth is intentionally kept lower than total company revenue growth, masking the true volume of the company's launch activities in its Space segment financials.

### 3. The Role of Launch Capacity in Enabling Starlink and Overall Revenue
While internal launches do not generate direct *Space segment* revenue, SpaceX describes launch capacity as the foundational "backbone" that enables the company's highly lucrative revenue streams in other segments:
*   **Driving Connectivity Revenue:** The Space segment's unparalleled launch capabilities provide the foundational strategic value necessary to scale the Connectivity segment. By deploying thousands of satellites, SpaceX generates "highly predictable and recurring subscription revenue" from millions of Starlink broadband and mobile subscribers. 
*   **Enabling Next-Generation Tech (V3 Satellites):** Future increases in launch capacity (specifically via the heavy-lift Starship vehicle) are directly tied to future revenue capabilities. Starship will allow SpaceX to deploy next-generation V3 satellites, representing a "twenty-fold increase in Starlink downlink capacity deployed relative to a Falcon 9 launch," which will drastically improve network capacity and service quality.
*   **Fueling the AI Segment:** Massive payload capacity is also slated to underpin the deployment of orbital AI compute infrastructure, accelerating the company's newly acquired AI segment.

### Summary: The Self-Reinforcing Value Cycle
SpaceX views the relationship between mass to orbit and revenue through the lens of a **"self-reinforcing value creation cycle."** The Space segment leverages reusable rockets to drastically lower the cost of access to space. Rather than just selling this capacity to third parties, SpaceX uses its massive launch throughput to build out its own global infrastructure (Starlink). Success and scale in launching kilograms to orbit directly fuels the rapid growth, market dominance, and recurring cash flows of the Connectivity and AI segments, which in turn fund the next frontier of space innovation.

Based on the provided financial disclosures for the company's AI segment (which encompasses the X platform, Grok, and related services), revenue is recognized according to the following policies:

### 1. Advertising Revenue (X Platform)
*   **Timing of Recognition:** Revenue is recognized in the period the advertising is delivered. Delivery is evidenced by a user engaging with an ad on the platform in a manner that satisfies the specific engagement criteria selected by the advertiser. (These contracts are typically cancellable, short-term arrangements).
*   **Gross vs. Net Recognition (Principal vs. Agent):**
    *   **Direct Sales (Gross Basis):** For advertising sold directly through the X platform, the company acts as the **principal** because it controls the ad services prior to transfer, is responsible for serving the ads, and fulfills the engagement criteria. Therefore, revenue is recognized on a **gross basis**.
    *   **SSP Partners (Net Basis):** For advertising sold through Supply Side Platform (SSP) partners, the company acts as an **agent** because the SSP partner controls the inventory, has pricing discretion, and is primarily responsible for fulfilling the obligation. The company receives a percentage share of the gross ad spend and recognizes revenue on a **net basis**.

### 2. AI Solutions & Infrastructure Revenue
This category consists of subscriptions, data licensing, and API access, which are recognized as follows:

*   **Premium Subscriptions (X and Grok):** 
    *   Revenue is recognized **ratably** over the period of the subscription term, which typically ranges from month-to-month up to one year.
*   **Data Licensing Arrangements:** 
    *   These arrangements grant customers the right to access, search, and analyze historical and real-time intellectual property (IP) on the X platform.
    *   *Single Performance Obligation:* Revenue is generally recognized **ratably (straight-line)** over the period (ranging from month-to-month up to two years) as the customer consumes and benefits from the data. (Note: Historical IP may be satisfied at a single point in time, while future IP is satisfied over time).
    *   *Multiple Performance Obligations:* If an arrangement contains multiple obligations, revenue is allocated on a relative basis using directly observable standalone selling prices, and recognized as each specific performance obligation is satisfied.
*   **API Access to Grok Models:**
    *   *Stand-Ready Access:* Revenue is recognized **ratably** over the contract term (typically month-to-month or up to one year).
    *   *Usage-Based Arrangements:* Revenue is recognized **as services are consumed** by the customer.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here are the "Mass to Orbit" figures for FY2025 and Q1 2026, along with the KPI table showing the historical trends. 

*Note: While the company defines "Mass to Orbit" as the "total kilograms of payload," the reported financial and operational figures in the text are presented in **metric tons** (1 metric ton = 1,000 kilograms).*

### Mass to Orbit Figures

*   **Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026):** **556 metric tons** (556,000 kilograms)

### KPI Table: Space Segment Operating and Financial Data
The following unaudited table from the text illustrates the mass to orbit trends alongside launch cadence and segment profitability metrics:

| Metric | Q1 2026 *(Three Months Ended Mar 31, 2026)* | Q1 2025 *(Three Months Ended Mar 31, 2025)* | FY2025 *(Year Ended Dec 31, 2025)* | FY2024 *(Year Ended Dec 31, 2024)* | FY2023 *(Year Ended Dec 31, 2023)* |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mass to Orbit (in metric tons)** | **556** | **450** | **2,213** | **1,699** | **1,210** |
| **Launches (number)** | 40 | 38 | 170 | 138 | 98 |
| **Segment income (loss) from operations (in millions)** | $(662) | $(70) | $(657) | $21 | $(1) |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA (in millions)** | $(351) | $224 | $653 | $1,154 | $997 |

**Context on the Metrics:** 
The preamble notes that "Mass to Orbit" and "Launches" grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue because these operational metrics include the company's internal constellation deployments (such as Starlink satellites), from which SpaceX does not recognize inter-segment revenue.

Based on the provided text, the pricing model for Launch Services is based on **fixed-price contracts**. 

These contracts typically range in duration from **one to five years** and are used for both commercial customers and governmental agency space programs.

arguments.final_result:
## How Incremental Kilograms to Orbit Translate into Revenue by Segment

SpaceX tracks "Mass to Orbit" (total kilograms of payload deployed to orbit) as a primary operational KPI. In FY2025, SpaceX delivered **2,213 metric tons** to orbit (312 MT customer payloads; 1,901 MT internal payloads). In Q1 2026, it delivered **556 metric tons**. Critically, the filing explicitly states that mass to orbit and launch cadence grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue because the majority of mass delivered is internal Starlink constellation deployment, for which **no inter-segment revenue is recognized**.

### 1. Space Segment — Direct and Indirect Revenue Translation

**Direct revenue (external customers only):** Incremental kilograms delivered for third-party commercial and government customers translate directly into Space segment revenue through fixed-price launch services contracts (typically 1–5 years). Revenue is recognized at a **point in time** — upon successful launch or deployment of the customer's payload to its intended orbit. For government development contracts, revenue is recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method**.

**Indirect revenue (internal launches):** The vast majority of mass to orbit (~86% in FY2025) consists of internal Starlink satellite deployments. These launches generate **zero Space segment revenue**. Instead, launch costs are capitalized as satellites within Property, Plant & Equipment in the Connectivity segment and depreciated over their useful lives. Thus, incremental internal kilograms to orbit are a **cost input** to the Space segment but a **capacity enabler** for Connectivity and AI segment revenue.

### 2. Connectivity Segment — Kilograms as Infrastructure Deployment

Incremental kilograms of Starlink satellites delivered to orbit directly expand the constellation's network capacity, coverage, and service quality. This translates into revenue through:

- **Consumer & Enterprise subscriptions:** More satellites → better coverage/capacity → more subscribers and higher ARPU. Revenue recognized **over time** — ratably over the subscription period or based on data consumption.
- **Hardware/Product sales (Starlink kits):** Revenue recognized at a **point in time** upon delivery (or installation for enterprise).
- **Starshield (government networks):** Revenue recognized **over time** using the cost-to-cost method on multi-year fixed-price contracts.
- **Starlink Mobile (satellite-to-mobile):** Revenue recognized **over time** as services are consumed.

The filing notes that next-generation V3 satellites (deployable via Starship) will deliver a **twenty-fold increase in downlink capacity** per launch versus Falcon 9, meaning each incremental kilogram of V3 satellite mass to orbit will generate substantially more connectivity revenue than current-generation satellites.

### 3. AI Segment — Emerging Orbital Infrastructure Link

The AI segment (xAI, Grok, X platform) currently generates revenue primarily through:
- **Advertising (X platform):** Recognized when ads are delivered and user engagement criteria are met (point in time).
- **AI subscriptions & API access:** Recognized **ratably** over the subscription term or **as consumed** for usage-based arrangements.
- **Data licensing:** Recognized **ratably** over the contract term.

The filing describes a forward-looking plan to deploy **orbital AI compute infrastructure** using Starship's massive payload capacity. If realized, incremental kilograms to orbit would directly enable AI segment compute capacity, though this remains a **forward-looking statement** and is not yet reflected in historical financials.

---

## Connectivity-to-Space Revenue Ratio Calculation

| Period | Connectivity Revenue | Space Revenue | Ratio (Connectivity / Space) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| **FY2025** | $11,387M | $4,086M | **2.79x** |
| **Q1 2026** | $3,257M | $619M | **5.26x** |

**Calculations:**
- FY2025: $11,387 ÷ $4,086 = **2.79x**
- Q1 2026: $3,257 ÷ $619 = **5.26x**

---

## Trend Interpretation

The Connectivity-to-Space revenue ratio **nearly doubled** from 2.79x in FY2025 to 5.26x in Q1 2026, indicating a sharp acceleration in the divergence between the two segments' revenue trajectories. This trend is driven by several structural factors explicitly disclosed in the filing:

1. **Revenue recognition asymmetry:** The Space segment only recognizes revenue from external customer launches, while the bulk of its operational output (86%+ of mass to orbit) is internal Starlink deployment that flows through as Connectivity segment revenue over time via subscriptions.

2. **Connectivity revenue compounding:** Starlink's subscription-based model generates **recurring, predictable revenue** that compounds as the subscriber base grows. Each satellite launched (funded by Space segment costs) generates multi-year subscription revenue streams in the Connectivity segment.

3. **Space segment revenue compression in Q1 2026:** Space segment revenue declined from a FY2025 quarterly run-rate of ~$1,022M to $619M in Q1 2026, while Connectivity grew from a FY2025 quarterly run-rate of ~$2,847M to $3,257M. This suggests Q1 2026 had a lower proportion of external customer launches relative to internal constellation deployment.

4. **Strategic capacity allocation:** The filing states that the internal business "continues to absorb most of the growth in our launch capacity," meaning SpaceX is deliberately prioritizing constellation buildout over selling launch capacity to third parties — a rational economic decision given the ~5.26x revenue multiplier effect of allocating a kilogram to Starlink versus selling it to an external customer.

**Bottom line:** The widening ratio confirms that SpaceX's business model is fundamentally a **vertically integrated infrastructure play** — the Space segment functions as a cost center that enables the far larger and faster-growing Connectivity revenue stream. The Space segment's true economic value is not captured in its own revenue line but is instead embedded in the Connectivity segment's recurring subscription base.

---

**Note on recast financials:** All segment figures reflect the retrospectively recast consolidated entity under common-control accounting, including xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods presented. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (Filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## How Incremental Kilograms to Orbit Translate into Revenue by Segment

SpaceX tracks "Mass to Orbit" (total kilograms of payload deployed to orbit) as a primary operational KPI. In FY2025, SpaceX delivered **2,213 metric tons** to orbit (312 MT customer payloads; 1,901 MT internal payloads). In Q1 2026, it delivered **556 metric tons**. Critically, the filing explicitly states that mass to orbit and launch cadence grow more rapidly than Space segment revenue because the majority of mass delivered is internal Starlink constellation deployment, for which **no inter-segment revenue is recognized**.

### 1. Space Segment — Direct and Indirect Revenue Translation

**Direct revenue (external customers only):** Incremental kilograms delivered for third-party commercial and government customers translate directly into Space segment revenue through fixed-price launch services contracts (typically 1–5 years). Revenue is recognized at a **point in time** — upon successful launch or deployment of the customer's payload to its intended orbit. For government development contracts, revenue is recognized **over time** using the **cost-to-cost input method**.

**Indirect revenue (internal launches):** The vast majority of mass to orbit (~86% in FY2025) consists of internal Starlink satellite deployments. These launches generate **zero Space segment revenue**. Instead, launch costs are capitalized as satellites within Property, Plant & Equipment in the Connectivity segment and depreciated over their useful lives. Thus, incremental internal kilograms to orbit are a **cost input** to the Space segment but a **capacity enabler** for Connectivity and AI segment revenue.

### 2. Connectivity Segment — Kilograms as Infrastructure Deployment

Incremental kilograms of Starlink satellites delivered to orbit directly expand the constellation's network capacity, coverage, and service quality. This translates into revenue through:

- **Consumer & Enterprise subscriptions:** More satellites → better coverage/capacity → more subscribers and higher ARPU. Revenue recognized **over time** — ratably over the subscription period or based on data consumption.
- **Hardware/Product sales (Starlink kits):** Revenue recognized at a **point in time** upon delivery (or installation for enterprise).
- **Starshield (government networks):** Revenue recognized **over time** using the cost-to-cost method on multi-year fixed-price contracts.
- **Starlink Mobile (satellite-to-mobile):** Revenue recognized **over time** as services are consumed.

The filing notes that next-generation V3 satellites (deployable via Starship) will deliver a **twenty-fold increase in downlink capacity** per launch versus Falcon 9, meaning each incremental kilogram of V3 satellite mass to orbit will generate substantially more connectivity revenue than current-generation satellites.

### 3. AI Segment — Emerging Orbital Infrastructure Link

The AI segment (xAI, Grok, X platform) currently generates revenue primarily through:
- **Advertising (X platform):** Recognized when ads are delivered and user engagement criteria are met (point in time).
- **AI subscriptions & API access:** Recognized **ratably** over the subscription term or **as consumed** for usage-based arrangements.
- **Data licensing:** Recognized **ratably** over the contract term.

The filing describes a forward-looking plan to deploy **orbital AI compute infrastructure** using Starship's massive payload capacity. If realized, incremental kilograms to orbit would directly enable AI segment compute capacity, though this remains a **forward-looking statement** and is not yet reflected in historical financials.

---

## Connectivity-to-Space Revenue Ratio Calculation

| Period | Connectivity Revenue | Space Revenue | Ratio (Connectivity / Space) |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| **FY2025** | $11,387M | $4,086M | **2.79x** |
| **Q1 2026** | $3,257M | $619M | **5.26x** |

**Calculations:**
- FY2025: $11,387 ÷ $4,086 = **2.79x**
- Q1 2026: $3,257 ÷ $619 = **5.26x**

---

## Trend Interpretation

The Connectivity-to-Space revenue ratio **nearly doubled** from 2.79x in FY2025 to 5.26x in Q1 2026, indicating a sharp acceleration in the divergence between the two segments' revenue trajectories. This trend is driven by several structural factors explicitly disclosed in the filing:

1. **Revenue recognition asymmetry:** The Space segment only recognizes revenue from external customer launches, while the bulk of its operational output (86%+ of mass to orbit) is internal Starlink deployment that flows through as Connectivity segment revenue over time via subscriptions.

2. **Connectivity revenue compounding:** Starlink's subscription-based model generates **recurring, predictable revenue** that compounds as the subscriber base grows. Each satellite launched (funded by Space segment costs) generates multi-year subscription revenue streams in the Connectivity segment.

3. **Space segment revenue compression in Q1 2026:** Space segment revenue declined from a FY2025 quarterly run-rate of ~$1,022M to $619M in Q1 2026, while Connectivity grew from a FY2025 quarterly run-rate of ~$2,847M to $3,257M. This suggests Q1 2026 had a lower proportion of external customer launches relative to internal constellation deployment.

4. **Strategic capacity allocation:** The filing states that the internal business "continues to absorb most of the growth in our launch capacity," meaning SpaceX is deliberately prioritizing constellation buildout over selling launch capacity to third parties — a rational economic decision given the ~5.26x revenue multiplier effect of allocating a kilogram to Starlink versus selling it to an external customer.

**Bottom line:** The widening ratio confirms that SpaceX's business model is fundamentally a **vertically integrated infrastructure play** — the Space segment functions as a cost center that enables the far larger and faster-growing Connectivity revenue stream. The Space segment's true economic value is not captured in its own revenue line but is instead embedded in the Connectivity segment's recurring subscription base.

---

**Note on recast financials:** All segment figures reflect the retrospectively recast consolidated entity under common-control accounting, including xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods presented. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.
